title: Alice's Guildford — a Looking-Glass Town trail
date_relevance: Evergreen · optional Summer Solstice overlay (21 Jun)
blurb: Follow Lewis Carroll's Guildford — the town where he finished Through the Looking-Glass and where two bronze Alices still play. Five real landmarks, clues you solve on the spot.

start.name: Top of the High Street, by the George Abbot statue
start.directions: ~10 min walk from Guildford rail station (London Waterloo line). Nearest car parks: Castle (Sydenham Rd), Tunsgate Quarter, or the Friary.

stops.name: George Abbot Statue
stops.clue: A bronze man in archbishop's robes stands at the top of the High Street. Look closely at his hands — what is he holding?
stops.answer_hint: It's something you'd read — held open in his hand.
stops.story: George Abbot, born in Guildford in 1562, rose to become Archbishop of Canterbury. This bronze by Faith Winter was unveiled in 1993. He founded the almshouses just behind you — Abbot's Hospital — in 1619.

stops.name: The Guildhall Clock
stops.clue: Find the famous golden clock that juts right out over the High Street. A grateful clockmaker gave it to the town — what year is painted in big gold figures on it?
stops.answer_hint: Four digits in gold, in the 1680s.
stops.story: London clockmaker John Aylward gave the town this projecting gilt clock in 1683 — in return for being allowed to trade here. English oak case, copper dials, re-gilded in 23½-carat gold leaf. It's the symbol of Guildford.

stops.name: Guildford Castle & the Looking-Glass Alice
stops.clue: Climb up to the Castle gardens. Tucked in a walled corner is a little bronze girl caught mid-step, doing the magical thing from the title of the book Lewis Carroll finished here. What is Alice stepping through?
stops.story: The Norman keep (built around the 1130s) towers over gardens opened to the public in 1888. In a walled corner stands Jeanne Argent's 1990 statue of Alice stepping through the mirror — Lewis Carroll completed 'Through the Looking-Glass' while staying with his sisters nearby on Castle Hill.

stops.name: St Mary's Church, Quarry Street
stops.clue: This little church on Quarry Street has the oldest stones in all of Guildford — and it's where Lewis Carroll's funeral was held. Its rough flint tower was raised around the year 1040. Which century does that make it?
stops.answer_hint: Built around 1040 — so it's the 11-hundreds minus one… count the hundreds.
stops.story: St Mary's Anglo-Saxon tower dates to about 1040, making it the oldest surviving building in Guildford — roughly 70 years older than the Castle. Lewis Carroll's funeral was held here in January 1898.

stops.name: Alice & the White Rabbit
stops.clue: Down by the River Wey, a bronze Alice kneels on the grass, following someone in a terrible hurry. Who is she chasing?
stops.story: Edwin Russell's bronze, unveiled in 1984, shows Alice following the White Rabbit while her sister reads — echoing the riverboat afternoon that first sparked the tale. A lovely riverside finish.

finale.title: You've found your way through Wonderland! 🐇
finale.message: Five stops, a thousand years of Guildford, and the town where Alice was dreamed up. Charles Dodgson — Lewis Carroll — knew these streets well.
finale.reward_idea: An ice cream by the River Wey, or a cream tea back up the High Street — you've earned it.

_recce_notes: FOOT-VERIFY these GPS pins on the first walk and tighten geofences: Stop 3 Looking-Glass Alice (in walled garden, tree cover — coord is the keep, 60 m fence covers grounds), Stop 4 St Mary's (Wikipedia coord only 4 dp), Stop 5 White Rabbit (riverside statue not individually geotagged — 70 m fence). Stops 1 & 2 coords are high-confidence.
